arcee.summary: Arcee models are best treated as practical open-model assistants for structured drafting, extraction, and cost-aware automation.
strengths: Good fit for repeatable business prompts
Useful when cost and control matter
weaknesses: May trail frontier models on deep reasoning
Requires prompt discipline for complex tool plans
Validate niche domain claims

bge.summary: BGE models are retrieval infrastructure models focused on embeddings, reranking, and search quality rather than conversational output.
weaknesses: Not intended for chat answers
Quality depends on chunking and evaluation
Latency depends on batch size and index design

claude.summary: Claude-family models are suited to careful writing, review, planning, and nuanced long-form reasoning when the account key allows access.
strengths: Good for review and explanation-heavy work
weaknesses: Access may vary by key
Can be more expensive than smaller models
Latency can be higher on large prompts

deepseek.summary: DeepSeek models are strong cost-performance choices for coding, reasoning, and practical automation workflows.
strengths: Good value on code and technical prompts
Often competitive for everyday engineering work
weaknesses: Verify safety-critical or regulatory claims
Style can be direct and terse
Availability and access can vary by catalog state

e5.summary: E5 models are embedding-focused Microsoft-origin retrieval models for search, clustering, and knowledge workflows.
strengths: Good fit for stable indexing pipelines
weaknesses: Not a conversational model
Needs retrieval evals before production use
Prompt formatting matters for some embedding tasks

flux.summary: FLUX models are image-generation models known for prompt-driven visual composition and creative exploration.
strengths: Useful for generated assets and mockups
weaknesses: Not useful for text chat
Precise text rendering may need retries
Cost and latency vary with image size

gemma.summary: Gemma models are Google-origin open models for efficient assistant, summarization, and general text workflows.
weaknesses: May lag larger frontier models
Needs evals for specialized domains
Not always ideal for complex coding agents

general.summary: General models should be evaluated by live routing, observed cost, latency, and output quality before being promoted as defaults.
strengths: Automatically available through the global registry
Can be promoted after access verification
weaknesses: Limited curated provider guidance
Needs hands-on validation
May have incomplete pricing or access metadata

glm.summary: GLM models are broad Chinese-origin assistants useful for analysis, writing, multilingual work, and long-context synthesis.
strengths: Strong option for comparative reasoning tests
weaknesses: Validate facts in regulated domains
Coding quality varies by model size
Access depends on provider catalog state

gpt.summary: GPT-family models are broad general-purpose assistants for coding, structured output, tool use, and production chat workflows when access is enabled.
strengths: Good fit for tool and schema-driven prompts
Easy to compare against Claude and DeepSeek baselines
weaknesses: Key access can block specific variants
Higher-end models can cost more
Use traces to verify requested versus routed model

gte.summary: GTE models are Alibaba-origin embedding models for English and multilingual retrieval workloads.
strengths: Useful companion to Qwen text models
weaknesses: Not a chat model
Quality depends on corpus and chunking
Needs recall and precision evals

kimi.summary: Kimi models are useful for long-form analysis, writing, and multilingual assistant workflows.
strengths: Useful alternative to GLM and Qwen
weaknesses: Validate technical edge cases
May not be the first choice for agentic coding
Access and pricing should be confirmed

llama.summary: Llama models are Meta-origin open models that work well as general assistant, summarization, and baseline comparison options.
strengths: Useful where open-model behavior is preferred
weaknesses: Frontier reasoning varies by size
Coding quality is model-dependent
May need stronger prompts for strict formats

mimo.summary: MiMo models are efficient Xiaomi-origin options for lightweight chat, fast drafting, and low-cost experimentation.
weaknesses: Not the best default for deep reasoning
Validate complex code changes
Use stronger models for high-stakes review

minimax.summary: MiniMax models are practical general assistants for chat, drafting, and comparative model-routing experiments.
strengths: Can be cost-effective depending on catalog pricing
weaknesses: Needs local evals before default routing
May be less predictable on strict technical formats
Access can vary

mistral.summary: Mistral models are concise, fast general-purpose assistants for drafting, summarization, and efficient production chat.
strengths: Good latency and cost profile on smaller variants
Strong candidate for default fallback routing
weaknesses: May need larger peers for deep reasoning
Can be terse when more exploration is desired
Compare against DeepSeek for coding-heavy work

nemotron.summary: Nemotron models are NVIDIA-origin instruction models useful for structured enterprise prompts, extraction, and controlled assistant behavior.
strengths: Strong comparison option for Llama-class tasks
weaknesses: May not be best for creative writing
Needs evals for long tool plans
Cost and access depend on provider catalog

qwen.summary: Qwen models are strong multilingual coding and reasoning options, especially when a Dedicated Inference server is available.
strengths: Dedicated variants can become the preferred route when online
weaknesses: Dedicated servers need lifecycle and cost controls
Validate safety-critical claims
Smaller variants may trade depth for speed

stable diffusion.summary: Stable Diffusion models are image-generation options for visual ideation, asset exploration, and iterative prompt development.
strengths: Works well with prompt builder workflows
weaknesses: Not a text chat model
Precise text and layout may need retries
Output should be reviewed before brand use
